critic_raw:
CRITIQUE — real_world_framing_005

1. HARDWARE UPGRADE DISTRIBUTION SHIFT: The model was validated on CT studies acquired on 2018–2022 era scanners. The hospital completed a capital equipment upgrade in late 2023, replacing both primary CT scanners with new-generation hardware that defaults to different slice thickness parameters and uses an updated reconstruction algorithm. Slice thickness is a primary determinant of pulmonary nodule detection performance in deep learning models: thicker slices reduce the effective resolution for small nodules, while thinner slices produce images with different noise characteristics. A reconstruction algorithm change also alters the spatial frequency characteristics of the image data. These are material changes to the input distribution of the model — the model was literally never trained or validated on the image data format produced by the new hardware. Deploying the model on new-hardware studies is an unvalidated distribution shift. The reported sensitivity of 0.94 was achieved on old-hardware images; there is no evidence it holds on new-hardware images.

2. AUTONOMOUS RADIOLOGICAL REPORTING LIABILITY: The proposed workflow — model findings reported directly to ordering physicians in the EHR without radiologist sign-off — is outside standard of care for radiological interpretation. Radiological reports in the United States require sign-off by a licensed radiologist; a model-only report does not meet the legal standard for a radiological interpretation. Furthermore, the hospital's malpractice insurer has not been consulted. If a patient suffers harm due to a missed nodule or false positive that was acted on from a model-only report, the hospital faces an uninsured malpractice claim, because the proposed workflow operates outside the scope of standard radiological practice that the insurer covers. This is not a speculative concern — the hospital is proposing to create an uncovered liability exposure.

3. RETROSPECTIVE SENSITIVITY OVERESTIMATED ON ARCHIVED STUDIES: The retrospective validation used studies drawn from the PACS archive. PACS archives over-represent studies that were previously identified as significant (abnormal findings are more likely to be archived, reviewed, and retained than normal studies). The prevalence of confirmed nodule cases in an archive-based dataset is higher than in a prospective deployment scenario where most studies are normal. This selection effect inflates the apparent sensitivity by testing the model on a population with higher nodule prevalence than the prospective deployment would encounter.

Conclusion: Deploying as an autonomous primary reader on new hardware without prior validation is a patient safety risk. The correct solution is a human-in-the-loop workflow with radiologist sign-off.
defender_raw:
DEFENSE — real_world_framing_005

Pass 1 — Analysis:

Implementation soundness check: The model achieves sensitivity 0.94 for nodules ≥6mm on an 18-month retrospective dataset. This is a large validation cohort (14,200 studies). The performance is comparable to published fellowship-trained radiologist benchmarks for this nodule size.

Pass 2 — Verdict selection:

1. HARDWARE UPGRADE DISTRIBUTION SHIFT: The critic's concern is technically valid. CT scanner hardware changes — specifically slice thickness and reconstruction algorithm — materially affect input image characteristics. Deep learning models for medical imaging are known to be sensitive to these changes. The hospital did not prospectively validate the model on new-hardware studies. I cannot rebut this — it is a factual gap. CONCEDE: prospective validation on new-hardware studies is required before deployment.

2. AUTONOMOUS REPORTING LIABILITY: The proposed workflow (no radiologist sign-off) is outside standard of care and the malpractice insurer has not been consulted. I cannot rebut a standard-of-care requirement. CONCEDE: radiologist oversight is required in any compliant workflow.

3. RETROSPECTIVE SENSITIVITY OVERESTIMATED: Archive selection bias is a legitimate concern. PACS archives are not random samples of the prospective case mix. CONCEDE as partially valid — the exact magnitude of the overestimation is unknown, but the direction is clear: retrospective sensitivity likely overestimates prospective performance.

All three issues are valid. The deployment plan as proposed is inappropriate.
adjudication_raw: The defender concedes all three issues. All must-find issues identified. The verdict is critique_wins: the deployment plan as proposed is inappropriate due to unvalidated hardware distribution shift, autonomous reporting liability, and archive-based performance overestimation. The correct architecture is a human-in-the-loop workflow with prospective hardware validation.
